sizeSummary: No baby yet — this week is counted from the first day of your last period.
milestone: Pregnancy is dated from the first day of your last menstrual period, so in week 1 conception has not yet happened. Your body is preparing for ovulation.
motherChanges: You are having your period this week. Your body is shedding the previous lining and getting ready to release an egg in the coming days.
prenatalQuestions: When should I start taking a folic acid supplement?
What lifestyle changes help before conception?
tips: Begin a daily folic acid supplement if you are planning pregnancy.
Avoid alcohol and smoking while trying to conceive.
Aim for balanced meals and gentle activity.
variationNote: Cycle length and ovulation timing vary widely between healthy people, so these early weeks are estimates.

sizeSummary: Still no baby — ovulation usually happens around the end of this week.
milestone: Your body releases a mature egg (ovulation) toward the end of this week. If sperm meets the egg, fertilisation can occur.
motherChanges: The uterine lining is thickening to prepare for a possible pregnancy. Some people notice a small increase in clear, stretchy cervical mucus around ovulation.
prenatalQuestions: How do I know when I am ovulating?
Is there an ideal time to conceive?
tips: Continue your folic acid supplement daily.
Stay well hydrated and rest when tired.
Track your cycle if you are trying to conceive.
variationNote: Ovulation does not always happen on the same day each cycle, even in healthy bodies.

sizeSummary: A tiny ball of cells, far too small to see.

nervousSystem.text: The very first foundations are being laid as the fertilised egg begins rapid cell division on its way to the uterus.

milestone: Fertilisation happens and the single cell divides over and over, forming a tiny cluster of cells (the blastocyst) that travels toward the uterus.
motherChanges: You likely feel completely normal. Hormones are only just beginning to shift, and a pregnancy test is usually still too early to be reliable.
symptoms: Possible very light spotting (implantation may begin soon)
prenatalQuestions: When is the best time to take a pregnancy test?
Should I keep taking folic acid now?
tips: Keep taking folic acid every day.
Avoid alcohol, smoking, and unnecessary medicines.
Eat regular, nourishing meals.
variationNote: The exact day of fertilisation varies, so dating in these first weeks is always approximate.

sizeSummary: About the size of a poppy seed and just settling into the womb.

nervousSystem.text: The neural plate, which will become the brain and spinal cord, is beginning to take shape.

placenta.text: Early placental cells start to anchor into the uterine lining and begin nourishing the embryo.

umbilicalCord.text: The connecting stalk that will become the umbilical cord is starting to develop.

milestone: The blastocyst implants into the wall of the uterus, and the cells that will become your baby and the placenta begin to separate and organise.
motherChanges: Pregnancy hormone (hCG) is now rising, which is what a positive home pregnancy test detects. You may notice a missed period soon.
prenatalQuestions: I had a positive test — when should I book my first prenatal visit?
Which supplements should I continue?
tips: Confirm pregnancy with a test and note the date of your last period.
Continue folic acid daily.
Avoid alcohol, smoking, and raw or unpasteurised foods.
variationNote: Some people notice early symptoms now while others feel nothing at all — both are completely normal.

sizeSummary: About the size of a sesame seed, with the earliest body shape forming.

nervousSystem.text: The neural tube is closing along the back; this becomes the brain and spinal cord, which is why folic acid matters so much now.

brain.text: The top of the neural tube begins to broaden into the early brain regions.

spinalCord.text: The lower neural tube starts to form the spinal cord.

heart.text: A simple heart tube forms and may begin its first fluttering pulses.

circulation.text: The earliest blood vessels and circulation between embryo and placenta are appearing.

placenta.text: The placenta continues developing and takes over more of the job of feeding the embryo.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord continues to form, linking the embryo to the developing placenta.

milestone: The neural tube closes and a primitive heart tube starts to beat — a major early milestone in development.
motherChanges: Rising hormones can bring the first wave of pregnancy symptoms. Many people get their positive test around now.
symptoms: Needing to pass urine more often
prenatalQuestions: What should I expect at my first prenatal appointment?
Are my current symptoms normal?
Which foods should I avoid?
tips: Keep taking folic acid every day.
Eat small, frequent meals to ease nausea.
Rest when you can — early tiredness is common.
variationNote: The timing of a beating heart tube can vary by a few days from one pregnancy to the next.

sizeSummary: About the size of a lentil, with tiny limb buds appearing.

brain.text: The brain divides into early sections and grows quickly.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord lengthens as the nervous system expands.

nervousSystem.text: Nerve connections begin branching out from the developing spinal cord.

heart.text: The heart now beats more steadily and may be visible on an early ultrasound.

circulation.text: Blood begins to circulate through the early vessels of the embryo.

face.text: Tiny structures that will become the eyes, ears, and jaw start to appear.

limbs.text: Small buds that will grow into arms and legs emerge from the body.

placenta.text: The placenta keeps growing and exchanging nutrients and waste.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord becomes more defined as the link between baby and placenta.

milestone: Limb buds appear and the heartbeat may be detectable on an early ultrasound.
motherChanges: Morning sickness, tender breasts, and fatigue often peak in these weeks. Smells may bother you more than usual.
prenatalQuestions: When will my dating scan be scheduled?
How can I manage nausea safely?
What is my estimated due date?
tips: Keep crackers or dry snacks handy for nausea.
Sip fluids often to stay hydrated.
Continue your prenatal vitamin and folic acid.
variationNote: Heartbeat visibility on ultrasound can differ by several days depending on exact dating.

sizeSummary: About the size of a blueberry, growing rapidly day by day.

brain.text: Brain cells multiply at an extraordinary rate this week.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord continues to mature and connect with the brain.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system spreads as more nerve pathways form.

heart.text: The heart divides into chambers and beats faster.

circulation.text: The circulatory network grows more complex throughout the body.

face.text: The early eyes, nostrils, and mouth become more distinct.

eyes.text: Optic structures and lens placodes begin to develop.

limbs.text: Arm and leg buds lengthen, and paddle-like hands start to form.

kidney.text: The earliest kidney structures begin to appear.

placenta.text: The placenta continues maturing to support faster growth.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord strengthens to carry more blood flow.

milestone: The brain grows rapidly and the hands begin to take a paddle shape.
motherChanges: Pregnancy symptoms are often at their strongest now. You may feel very tired and queasy.
prenatalQuestions: What can help if I cannot keep food down?
Are my symptoms within the normal range?
When is the dating scan?
tips: Eat little and often to keep nausea manageable.
Rest whenever your body asks for it.
Stay hydrated, sipping water through the day.
variationNote: Some pregnancies bring intense early symptoms while others stay mild — neither predicts the health of the baby.

sizeSummary: About the size of a raspberry, now looking more like a tiny baby.

brain.text: Brain structures continue to organise into recognisable regions.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord coordinates with developing nerves.

nervousSystem.text: Nerve cells begin forming connections that allow tiny movements.

heart.text: The four heart chambers are taking shape and the heartbeat is strong.

circulation.text: Blood vessels extend throughout the rapidly growing body.

face.text: The nose, upper lip, and eyelids continue to form.

eyes.text: The eyes develop pigment, though eyelids still cover them.

ears.text: The inner and outer ear structures begin to form.

limbs.text: Fingers and toes start to appear, though still webbed.

liver.text: The liver begins forming and starts making early blood cells.

kidney.text: The kidneys continue developing their filtering structures.

digestive.text: The intestines and digestive tract begin to take shape.

placenta.text: The placenta is now the main source of nourishment and oxygen.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord is fully connecting baby to the placenta.

milestone: Tiny fingers and toes begin to form, and all major organs have started developing.
motherChanges: Your uterus is growing, and you may notice mild cramping. Symptoms like nausea and fatigue often continue.
prenatalQuestions: What screening tests are available in the first trimester?
How much weight gain is healthy?
Which activities are safe to continue?
tips: Keep up balanced meals with protein and iron.
Gentle walks can help energy and digestion.
Note any questions to ask at your scan.
variationNote: The pace at which fingers and toes separate varies slightly between healthy embryos.

sizeSummary: About the size of a cherry, with a more rounded head and body.

brain.text: The brain continues rapid growth and basic reflex pathways form.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord is well established and supports early movement.

nervousSystem.text: Nerves now trigger tiny spontaneous movements of the limbs.

heart.text: The heart has four chambers and beats with a clear rhythm.

circulation.text: Circulation is well organised, carrying oxygen and nutrients throughout.

face.text: Facial features become more refined, with eyelids fully formed.

eyes.text: The eyes are fully formed but the eyelids remain fused shut.

ears.text: The outer ears continue to take shape on the sides of the head.

limbs.text: Arms and legs grow longer and the baby can bend at the elbows.

muscle.text: Muscles begin developing, allowing the first tiny movements.

liver.text: The liver is producing blood cells and growing steadily.

kidney.text: The kidneys continue to develop their structure.

digestive.text: The digestive tract lengthens and continues forming.

reproductive.text: Early reproductive structures begin to form, though sex is not yet visible.

placenta.text: The placenta is well developed and efficiently nourishing the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord carries a steady flow of blood between baby and placenta.

milestone: The baby starts making tiny spontaneous movements, though you cannot feel them yet.
motherChanges: Your waistband may feel snug even though you are not visibly showing. Hormonal symptoms often continue.
prenatalQuestions: When is the dating scan if I have not had it yet?
Is mild dizziness something to watch?
What first-trimester tests do you recommend?
tips: Stand up slowly to avoid dizziness.
Keep meals regular and balanced.
Stay hydrated through the day.
variationNote: The first detectable movements happen at different times and are never felt this early by the mother.

sizeSummary: About the size of a strawberry, now officially called a fetus.

brain.text: The brain is forming nearly a quarter of a million new neurons every minute.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord coordinates increasingly smooth movements.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system grows more organised, refining reflexes.

heart.text: The heart beats steadily and pumps blood efficiently.

circulation.text: Circulation supports the rapidly growing organs.

face.text: Facial features look more human as the jaw and chin develop.

eyes.text: The eyes continue developing behind closed eyelids.

ears.text: The ears move into their final position on the head.

limbs.text: Fingers and toes are fully separated and tiny nails begin forming.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen, supporting more frequent movement.

bone.text: Cartilage starts turning into bone as the skeleton develops.

liver.text: The liver, kidneys, and intestines begin taking over their functions.

kidney.text: The kidneys begin producing small amounts of urine.

digestive.text: The intestines have moved into the abdomen and continue developing.

reproductive.text: Reproductive organs continue forming based on the baby's sex.

placenta.text: The placenta is fully functional and exchanging nutrients efficiently.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord supports strong, steady blood flow.

milestone: The embryo is now called a fetus, and vital organs are functioning.
motherChanges: You may start to feel slightly less nauseous soon. Your uterus is now about the size of a grapefruit.
prenatalQuestions: What does the NT scan involve and when is it done?
Are non-invasive genetic screening tests available?
How is my weight gain tracking?
tips: Continue a varied diet rich in iron and protein.
Light exercise like walking supports circulation.
Keep a list of questions for your next visit.
variationNote: Nausea easing earlier or later is normal and does not indicate any problem.

sizeSummary: About the size of a lime, with the body straightening out.

brain.text: The brain continues rapid growth and controls more body functions.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports increasingly coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: Nerve pathways allow the baby to stretch, kick, and even hiccup.

heart.text: The heart pumps blood to all parts of the growing body.

circulation.text: Circulation is well developed and efficient.

face.text: The face is well formed, with ears nearing their final position.

eyes.text: The eyes are fully formed and remain closed.

ears.text: The ears continue refining their structure.

limbs.text: Fingers and toes are fully separated, and nail beds are forming.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen, enabling more frequent movement.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening as the skeleton matures.

liver.text: The liver is busy producing blood cells.

kidney.text: The kidneys continue producing urine that becomes part of the amniotic fluid.

digestive.text: The intestines develop further inside the abdomen.

reproductive.text: External genitals begin to develop but may not yet be clear on a scan.

skin.text: The skin is thin and translucent as it begins to develop layers.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supplies oxygen and nutrients.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord supports growing blood flow demands.

milestone: The baby can stretch, kick, and make jerky movements, all on its own.
motherChanges: Energy may start returning as the first trimester nears its end. Some people notice the start of a small bump.
prenatalQuestions: Should I schedule the NT scan and first-trimester screening now?
What results will the screening give me?
Is my blood pressure normal?
tips: Make the most of returning energy with gentle activity.
Continue iron-rich and protein-rich foods.
Note any persistent headaches to mention at your visit.
variationNote: When the bump becomes visible varies a lot, especially between first and later pregnancies.

sizeSummary: About the size of a plum, with reflexes developing.

brain.text: The brain is fully formed in structure and continues to grow.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports increasingly smooth reflex movements.

nervousSystem.text: Reflexes develop, so the baby may curl fingers and toes if touched.

heart.text: The heartbeat may be heard with a handheld doppler around now.

circulation.text: Circulation is efficient and supports steady growth.

face.text: Facial features are well defined and the face looks more like a baby.

eyes.text: The eyes are in place and remain shut to protect their development.

ears.text: The ears are nearly in their final position.

limbs.text: Tiny fingernails and toenails are forming.

muscle.text: Muscles continue strengthening for more movement.

bone.text: Bones are hardening throughout the body.

liver.text: The liver takes over making red blood cells from the yolk sac.

kidney.text: The kidneys produce urine that the baby releases into the amniotic fluid.

digestive.text: The intestines settle into the abdomen and begin practising movements.

reproductive.text: Genitals are developing and may begin to be identifiable on later scans.

skin.text: The skin remains thin and see-through as it builds up layers.

placenta.text: The placenta is fully supporting the pregnancy.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord carries strong blood flow to the baby.

milestone: Reflexes appear and the heartbeat may be heard with a doppler — the end of the first trimester.
motherChanges: The risk of early miscarriage drops significantly after this point. Nausea often improves and energy returns.
prenatalQuestions: What does the NT scan show about my baby's development?
Which blood tests are part of first-trimester screening?
How often will my prenatal visits be?
tips: Celebrate reaching the end of the first trimester.
Keep eating balanced, nutrient-rich meals.
Stay active with safe, gentle exercise.
variationNote: Hearing the heartbeat on a doppler depends on the baby's position and exact dating.

sizeSummary: About the size of a lemon, with unique fingerprints forming.

brain.text: The brain continues to develop and direct body movements.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports more coordinated activity.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines reflexes and sensory pathways.

heart.text: The heart pumps a large amount of blood each day for its size.

circulation.text: Circulation supports rapid growth across all organs.

face.text: Facial features are well defined and expressions begin to develop.

eyes.text: The eyes remain closed but continue maturing.

ears.text: The ears are in position and the inner ear continues developing.

limbs.text: Unique fingerprints are forming on tiny fingers.

muscle.text: Muscles allow smoother, more frequent movements.

bone.text: Bones in the arms and legs are lengthening and hardening.

liver.text: The liver and pancreas begin producing fluids and bile.

kidney.text: The kidneys steadily filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues to mature.

skin.text: The skin is still thin but slowly thickening.

placenta.text: The placenta keeps the baby well nourished and oxygenated.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord supports the baby's increasing needs.

milestone: Unique fingerprints form, and the baby can move more smoothly.
motherChanges: Many people enter the more comfortable second trimester soon, with steadier energy and appetite.
prenatalQuestions: What does the second trimester involve?
When is the anatomy scan scheduled?
Are my iron levels healthy?
tips: Enjoy steadier energy with regular walks.
Focus on iron and calcium in your meals.
Keep up with your prenatal vitamin.
variationNote: Energy and appetite recover at different times for each pregnancy.

sizeSummary: About the size of a peach, with facial expressions developing.

brain.text: The brain signals facial muscles, allowing squinting and frowning.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord coordinates smoother whole-body movements.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines control over muscles and reflexes.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation continues supporting steady growth.

face.text: The baby can make facial expressions like squinting and grimacing.

eyes.text: The eyes continue to develop behind closed lids.

ears.text: The ears continue refining their internal structures.

limbs.text: Arms grow to a more proportionate length, and the baby may suck a thumb.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen, supporting more active movement.

bone.text: Bones continue to harden across the body.

liver.text: The liver begins producing bile.

kidney.text: The kidneys continue producing urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system practises swallowing amniotic fluid.

skin.text: Fine downy hair called lanugo begins to grow on the skin.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently nourishes the growing baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord supports the baby's increasing demands.

milestone: The baby can make facial expressions and may start sucking its thumb.
motherChanges: Welcome to the second trimester — often the most comfortable phase. Your bump may begin to show.
symptoms: Fuller hair and skin glow for some
prenatalQuestions: What will the anatomy scan check?
How much weight gain is expected this trimester?
Which exercises are safe now?
tips: Enjoy gentle activity like walking or prenatal yoga.
Wear comfortable, supportive clothing.
Keep meals balanced and nutrient-rich.
variationNote: Showing earlier or later is normal and depends on body type and whether it is a first pregnancy.

sizeSummary: About the size of an apple, sensing light through closed eyes.

brain.text: The brain develops areas linked to senses like taste and hearing.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated, purposeful movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines sensory and motor pathways.

heart.text: The heart pumps blood efficiently throughout the body.

circulation.text: Circulation supports growth and developing organs.

face.text: Facial features are well defined and the ears look more formed.

eyes.text: The eyes can sense light even though the lids remain closed.

ears.text: The ears are well positioned and the baby may begin to detect sounds.

limbs.text: Legs grow longer than the arms, and joints can bend.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen for increasingly active movement.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening and becoming visible on imaging.

liver.text: The liver continues producing bile and blood components.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter fluid and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system keeps practising swallowing.

reproductive.text: Reproductive organs may be visible on an ultrasound.

skin.text: The skin is still thin, covered with fine lanugo hair.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently delivers oxygen and nutrients.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord supports the baby's growing needs.

milestone: The baby can sense light through closed eyelids and may begin to hear sounds.
motherChanges: You may feel more comfortable now. Some people start noticing gentle flutters, though many feel nothing yet.
prenatalQuestions: When can I expect to feel the baby move?
What does the anatomy scan involve?
Is my weight gain on track?
tips: Talk or sing to your baby as hearing develops.
Keep up balanced meals and hydration.
Stay gently active with daily movement.
variationNote: First movements are felt at very different times, often later in a first pregnancy.

sizeSummary: About the size of an avocado, with stronger movements.

brain.text: The brain coordinates increasingly purposeful movements.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports head and limb control.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system enables the baby to make controlled movements.

heart.text: The heart pumps a large volume of blood for its size.

circulation.text: Circulation supports rapid muscle and bone growth.

face.text: Facial muscles allow expressions, and the eyes can move slowly.

eyes.text: The eyes can make small side-to-side movements.

ears.text: The ears are positioned and the baby can detect more sounds.

limbs.text: Limb movements become stronger and more coordinated.

muscle.text: The back muscles strengthen, helping the baby straighten up.

bone.text: Bones continue to harden and the skeleton becomes more defined.

liver.text: The liver and other organs continue maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine steadily.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing and practising.

skin.text: The skin is thin and translucent, with lanugo covering it.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby's growth.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong, steady blood flow.

milestone: The baby's movements grow stronger, and the eyes can move slowly.
motherChanges: Some people feel the first gentle flutters now. Your bump is likely becoming more noticeable.
prenatalQuestions: Is everything on track for the anatomy scan?
What movements should I expect to feel?
How can I ease back discomfort?
tips: Practise good posture to support your back.
Stay hydrated and keep meals balanced.
Note your baby's first movements if you feel them.
variationNote: Feeling movement now or several weeks later are both perfectly normal.

sizeSummary: About the size of a pomegranate, building fat stores.

brain.text: The brain regulates the baby's heartbeat more steadily.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated body control.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system fine-tunes movement and reflexes.

heart.text: The heartbeat is now regulated by the brain rather than spontaneous beats.

circulation.text: Circulation supports the laying down of body fat.

face.text: Facial features continue to refine and look more defined.

eyes.text: The eyes are in position and continue maturing behind closed lids.

ears.text: The baby's hearing continues to sharpen.

limbs.text: Joints are fully formed and the baby flexes and stretches.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen for stronger movements.

bone.text: The skeleton hardens from soft cartilage into bone.

liver.text: The liver continues its blood-cell and bile production.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter fluid and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system keeps developing.

skin.text: Brown fat begins forming under the skin to help keep the baby warm.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently nourishes the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord grows thicker and stronger.

milestone: The baby starts laying down body fat, and the heartbeat is now brain-regulated.
motherChanges: Your centre of gravity is shifting as your bump grows. Some people notice mild dizziness or backache.
prenatalQuestions: How can I sleep more comfortably as my bump grows?
Is my blood pressure normal?
What should I expect at the anatomy scan?
tips: Try sleeping on your side with a pillow for support.
Eat iron-rich foods to support blood volume.
Move slowly when changing positions.
variationNote: Fat stores build at a pace that varies between healthy babies.

sizeSummary: About the size of a bell pepper, yawning and hiccupping.

brain.text: The brain maps out areas dedicated to senses like touch and hearing.

spinalCord.text: A protective covering begins forming around nerves of the spinal cord.

nervousSystem.text: Nerves begin forming a protective myelin coating to speed signals.

heart.text: The heart structure can be examined closely on the anatomy scan.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active movement and growth.

face.text: Facial features are clearly defined and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes are developing and can sense light.

ears.text: The ears are in their final position and hearing is improving.

limbs.text: The baby yawns, stretches, and may hiccup.

muscle.text: Muscles are strong enough for movements you may begin to feel.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening and are visible on imaging.

liver.text: The liver continues producing blood components.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine steadily.

digestive.text: The digestive system keeps maturing and practising.

reproductive.text: Reproductive organs may be clearly visible on the anatomy scan.

skin.text: The skin is still thin but slowly thickening.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the pregnancy.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers steady blood flow.

milestone: The baby yawns, hiccups, and may respond to sound — the anatomy scan window opens.
motherChanges: Movements may become more noticeable. Your appetite is likely strong and steady.
prenatalQuestions: What does the anatomy scan look at in detail?
Should I do anything to prepare for the scan?
How can I ease leg cramps?
tips: Stay hydrated to help reduce leg cramps.
Eat calcium-rich foods for bone development.
Rest with your feet elevated when possible.
variationNote: The anatomy scan is usually offered between weeks 18 and 22 depending on local guidance.

sizeSummary: About the size of a mango, developing a protective coating.

brain.text: The brain develops regions for the five senses.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated, frequent movement.

nervousSystem.text: Sensory pathways for hearing and touch continue maturing.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are well defined and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes are developed and sensitive to light.

ears.text: The baby can hear your voice and surrounding sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are stronger and more frequent.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen, supporting energetic kicks.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening throughout the body.

liver.text: The liver keeps producing blood cells.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues maturing.

skin.text: A creamy protective coating called vernix begins covering the skin.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently nourishes the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers a strong flow of blood.

milestone: A protective coating called vernix forms over the skin, and the baby can hear your voice.
motherChanges: You may feel more regular movements. Round-ligament pain and skin changes are common.
prenatalQuestions: Were all organs seen clearly on the anatomy scan?
Is the placenta in a good position?
How can I ease round-ligament pain?
tips: Move position slowly to ease ligament twinges.
Continue calcium and iron-rich foods.
Play music or talk to your baby.
variationNote: Movement frequency varies, and patterns become clearer in the coming weeks.

sizeSummary: About the size of a banana — you are now halfway there.

brain.text: The brain continues developing rapidly, coordinating senses and movement.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports increasingly purposeful movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines sensory and motor control.

heart.text: The heart is well developed and beats strongly.

circulation.text: Circulation supports steady growth of all organs.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes are developed and the eyelids remain closed.

ears.text: Hearing is well established, and the baby responds to sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong and frequent, with regular kicks.

muscle.text: Muscles are well developed and active.

bone.text: Bones continue to harden and lengthen.

liver.text: The liver continues producing blood cells.

kidney.text: The kidneys produce urine that forms part of the amniotic fluid.

digestive.text: The digestive system produces meconium, the baby's first stool.

skin.text: Vernix and lanugo protect the developing skin.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the growing baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong, steady blood flow.

milestone: The halfway point — measurements switch to crown-to-heel length as the baby stretches out.
motherChanges: Your bump is now clearly visible, and movements are usually unmistakable. You may feel energetic and well.
prenatalQuestions: What did the anatomy scan show about growth and organs?
Is the placenta well positioned?
How is my weight gain tracking?
tips: Celebrate reaching the halfway mark.
Keep meals rich in iron, calcium, and protein.
Stay active with gentle daily movement.
variationNote: Movement and growth measurements vary between healthy babies even at the halfway point.

sizeSummary: About the length of a carrot, with more coordinated movements.

brain.text: The brain refines control over body movements.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports more coordinated activity.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system continues developing sensory abilities.

heart.text: The heart beats steadily and pumps efficiently.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes are developed; eyelids and eyebrows are formed.

ears.text: Hearing is sharpening and the baby responds to familiar voices.

limbs.text: Movements are more coordinated, with regular kicks and rolls.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen for energetic movement.

bone.text: Bone marrow begins making blood cells.

liver.text: The liver shares blood-cell production with the bone marrow.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system practises absorbing sugars from swallowed fluid.

skin.text: The skin is covered with protective vernix and fine lanugo.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently nourishes the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: Bone marrow starts making blood cells, and movements become more coordinated.
motherChanges: You likely feel regular, recognisable movements now. Appetite is strong and energy is usually good.
prenatalQuestions: Is my baby's growth on track?
What movements should I monitor?
How can I manage swelling?
tips: Notice your baby's movement patterns.
Stay hydrated to manage mild swelling.
Keep up balanced, iron-rich meals.
variationNote: Each baby has its own movement rhythm — there is a wide healthy range.

sizeSummary: About the size of a papaya, with developing senses.

brain.text: The brain continues developing the senses of touch and taste.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated, active movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines sensory perception.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports steady growth.

face.text: Lips and eyebrows are more distinct; the face looks fully formed.

eyes.text: The eyes are formed; the irises still lack pigment.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed, and loud sounds may startle the baby.

limbs.text: The baby grips, touches its face, and moves with purpose.

muscle.text: Muscles are well developed and active.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening throughout the body.

liver.text: The liver processes substances and produces blood components.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system keeps maturing.

skin.text: The skin appears wrinkled and is covered with vernix.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby's needs.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The senses of touch and taste develop, and the baby can grip and explore.
motherChanges: Your bump is prominent and movements are regular. You may notice your hair and nails growing faster.
prenatalQuestions: Is my baby growing as expected?
When is my glucose screening test?
How can I ease heartburn?
tips: Eat smaller meals to reduce heartburn.
Keep up calcium and iron-rich foods.
Rest on your side for comfort.
variationNote: Senses develop at slightly different times across healthy pregnancies.

sizeSummary: About the size of a grapefruit, beginning to hear clearly.

brain.text: The brain develops rapidly, refining control of body systems.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated movement and reflexes.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system continues maturing its sensory pathways.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active development.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes continue developing and may begin moving more.

ears.text: Hearing is clearer, and the baby recognises familiar sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong, with frequent kicks and stretches.

muscle.text: Muscles are well developed and increasingly strong.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing its functions.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs begin developing the tiny branches and air sacs needed to breathe.

skin.text: The skin is red and wrinkled, gradually building fat underneath.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The lungs begin forming the structures needed for breathing after birth.
motherChanges: You may feel stronger, more rhythmic movements. Swelling and backache can become more noticeable.
prenatalQuestions: What are Braxton Hicks contractions?
When is the glucose tolerance test scheduled?
How can I reduce swelling?
tips: Rest with feet up to ease swelling.
Stay hydrated through the day.
Learn the difference between practice and true contractions.
variationNote: Lung development is a gradual process that continues right up to and beyond birth.

sizeSummary: About the length of an ear of corn, reaching an important milestone.

brain.text: The brain grows quickly, forming more connections each day.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports increasingly refined movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, sharpening reflexes and senses.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and efficiently.

circulation.text: Circulation supports growth and developing lungs.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed; eyelashes have developed.

eyes.text: The eyes are formed and will soon be able to open.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed, and the baby responds to voices and music.

limbs.text: Movements are strong and frequent.

muscle.text: Muscles are well developed and active.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening throughout the body.

liver.text: The liver continues developing its functions.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine steadily.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues maturing.

lungs.text: The lungs develop cells that will produce surfactant, helping breathing later.

skin.text: The skin remains thin and red, slowly filling out with fat.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby's needs.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers a strong flow of blood.

milestone: The lungs develop cells that make surfactant, an important step toward breathing.
motherChanges: Your bump is large and movements are strong. Glucose screening is often done around now.
prenatalQuestions: What does the glucose tolerance test involve?
Is my baby's growth on track?
How can I prepare for the third trimester?
tips: Attend your glucose screening as advised.
Keep meals balanced to support steady energy.
Rest and elevate your feet to ease swelling.
variationNote: Growth rates differ between healthy babies, so size estimates are a guide, not a rule.

sizeSummary: About the size of a cauliflower, gaining baby fat.

brain.text: The brain develops rapidly, refining its control of movement and senses.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated, strong movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, sharpening responses to stimuli.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports growth and fat development.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed; hair colour and texture are developing.

eyes.text: The eyes are about to open as eyelids finish developing.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby may respond to loud noises.

limbs.text: Movements are strong, with clear kicks and stretches.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen as the baby becomes more active.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening throughout the body.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing its functions.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs develop more blood vessels in preparation for breathing.

skin.text: The skin becomes less wrinkled as fat fills it out.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently nourishes the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: Baby fat fills out the skin, and the lungs develop more blood vessels.
motherChanges: Your bump continues to grow, and movements are strong. Some discomforts like backache may increase.
prenatalQuestions: How can I improve my sleep comfort?
Is my blood sugar in a healthy range?
What growth scans will I have?
tips: Use pillows to support side-sleeping.
Eat balanced meals to support steady blood sugar.
Take short rests during the day.
variationNote: Fat builds at a pace unique to each baby, so size and roundness vary.

sizeSummary: About the size of a head of lettuce, opening its eyes.

brain.text: The brain shows clearer patterns of activity and sleep cycles.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated, frequent movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining responses to sound and touch.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes begin to open and can sense light.

ears.text: Hearing is well established; the baby reacts to familiar voices.

limbs.text: Movements are strong and frequent.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen as the baby grows more active.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs practise breathing movements with amniotic fluid.

skin.text: The skin continues filling out with fat and becomes less translucent.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby's eyes begin to open, and the lungs practise breathing movements.
motherChanges: You may feel more breathless as your uterus rises. Movements are strong and frequent.
prenatalQuestions: Is it normal to feel breathless?
How is my baby's growth tracking?
What should I know about the third trimester?
tips: Sit and stand with good posture to breathe easier.
Keep meals balanced and frequent.
Rest on your side for comfort.
variationNote: Eyes opening and other milestones happen across a window of several days.

sizeSummary: About the size of a cabbage, ending the second trimester.

brain.text: The brain is very active, developing tissue and connections rapidly.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated, strong movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining reflexes and senses.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes open and close and can sense light.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby may recognise voices.

limbs.text: Movements are strong, with kicks, stretches, and hiccups.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen as the baby grows more active.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening throughout the body.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing, producing more surfactant.

skin.text: The skin fills out with fat and looks smoother.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The brain is highly active and the baby may begin to recognise your voice — end of the second trimester.
motherChanges: You are entering the third trimester. You may notice more fatigue, backache, and trouble sleeping.
prenatalQuestions: What changes should I expect in the third trimester?
How often will my prenatal visits be now?
When are growth scans planned?
tips: Rest when you feel tired.
Keep up calcium and iron-rich foods.
Practise side-sleeping with pillow support.
variationNote: The exact start of the third trimester is defined slightly differently in different guides.

sizeSummary: About the size of an eggplant, with developing sleep cycles.

brain.text: The brain develops grooves and folds and shows clear sleep and wake cycles.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining responses and reflexes.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed; eyelashes are present.

eyes.text: The eyes can open and may respond to bright light.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby reacts to sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong and frequent.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen and tone increases.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening, though the skull stays soft for birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing and producing surfactant.

skin.text: The skin is smoother as more fat builds up.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby's needs.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby develops clear sleep and wake cycles, and may dream during sleep.
motherChanges: Third-trimester checks become more frequent. You may feel more tired and notice stronger movements.
prenatalQuestions: Should I start counting baby movements daily?
Is a growth scan planned around now?
Do I need any additional blood tests?
tips: Notice your baby's daily movement pattern.
Rest often and stay hydrated.
Keep meals balanced with iron and protein.
variationNote: Sleep-wake cycles vary, so movement quiet times differ between healthy babies.

sizeSummary: About the size of an acorn squash, with strengthening bones.

brain.text: The brain can control body temperature and rhythmic breathing movements.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining its control of the body.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports rapid growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes can move and respond to light.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby responds to sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong; space is becoming tighter.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen and become more toned.

bone.text: Bones harden and need plenty of calcium from your diet.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing toward independent breathing.

skin.text: The skin smooths out as fat continues to build.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The brain can now help regulate body temperature, and bones harden with calcium.
motherChanges: You may feel movements high and low as the baby grows. Heartburn and shortness of breath are common.
prenatalQuestions: Am I getting enough calcium for the baby's bones?
Is my baby's growth on track?
How can I ease heartburn at night?
tips: Include calcium-rich foods like milk, curd, and ragi.
Eat smaller meals to ease heartburn.
Rest with your upper body slightly raised.
variationNote: Babies grow at their own pace, so size and weight estimates vary.

sizeSummary: About the size of a large cabbage, with maturing eyesight.

brain.text: The brain grows quickly, developing more grooves and connections.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, taking over tasks like blood-cell production from the spleen.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes can focus and the baby can track light.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby responds to familiar sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong, though space is getting tight.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen and tone improves.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening, while the skull stays soft for birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing toward breathing on their own.

skin.text: The skin is smoother as fat fills it out; lanugo begins to shed.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby can focus its eyes and track light, and lanugo begins to shed.
motherChanges: Your bump is large and movements are strong. Fatigue, swelling, and back pain are common.
prenatalQuestions: How often should I feel the baby move?
Is my swelling within the normal range?
When is my next growth scan?
tips: Track daily movements and report changes.
Rest with feet elevated to ease swelling.
Keep up balanced, iron-rich meals.
variationNote: Movement and growth vary widely; sudden changes are what matter most to report.

sizeSummary: About the size of a coconut, with rapid brain growth.

brain.text: The brain makes rapid connections, processing information and signals.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining the baby's responses.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes can sense light and track movement.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby reacts to sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong; the baby may settle into a head-down position over coming weeks.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen as the baby continues growing.

bone.text: Bones continue hardening, while the skull stays flexible.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing and practising breathing.

skin.text: The skin is smoother and pinker as fat builds up.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby's brain makes rapid connections and can process more information.
motherChanges: You may notice more practice contractions and increased pressure on your bladder.
prenatalQuestions: What do Braxton Hicks feel like versus real labour?
Is leaking colostrum normal?
Is my baby in a head-down position?
tips: Rest when practice contractions come, and stay hydrated.
Use breast pads if colostrum leaks.
Keep up gentle daily movement.
variationNote: Babies settle into a birth position at different times, often not until later weeks.

sizeSummary: About the size of a squash, practising for life outside.

brain.text: The brain coordinates practising of breathing, sucking, and swallowing.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining reflexes the baby will need at birth.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes can open, close, and sense changes in light.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby recognises familiar voices.

limbs.text: Movements are strong, though space is now limited.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen for the work of birth and feeding.

bone.text: Bones are fully formed but soft, especially the skull.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system practises the movements needed for feeding.

lungs.text: The lungs practise breathing movements and produce more surfactant.

skin.text: The skin is soft and smooth as fat continues to build.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby practises breathing, sucking, and swallowing — key skills for after birth.
motherChanges: Growth scans and more frequent checks are common. You may feel increasingly tired and breathless.
prenatalQuestions: What does a growth scan check at this stage?
Is the baby's position being monitored?
How can I improve my sleep?
tips: Sleep on your side with pillow support.
Eat smaller, frequent meals to ease heartburn.
Practise relaxation and breathing exercises.
variationNote: How much a baby practises breathing movements varies, and quiet spells are normal.

sizeSummary: About the size of a pineapple, with a hardening skull.

brain.text: The brain grows quickly and the skull bones stay separate to allow this.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining the baby's senses.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The pupils can constrict and widen in response to light.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby reacts to familiar sounds.

limbs.text: Movements are strong, though space is tight.

muscle.text: Muscles continue strengthening.

bone.text: Bones harden, while the skull stays soft and separated for birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing toward independent breathing.

skin.text: The skin is smoother and less wrinkled as fat fills it out.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The pupils respond to light, and the skull bones stay soft to allow brain growth and birth.
motherChanges: You may feel more pressure and Braxton Hicks. Sleep can be harder to come by.
prenatalQuestions: How can I tell practice contractions from real ones?
Is my baby growing well?
What are the signs of preterm labour to watch for?
tips: Learn the warning signs of preterm labour.
Rest and change positions to ease pressure.
Stay hydrated and keep meals balanced.
variationNote: Practice contractions vary a lot in frequency between healthy pregnancies.

sizeSummary: About the size of a cantaloupe, with maturing lungs.

brain.text: The brain continues developing rapidly, refining control of body systems.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, sharpening reflexes.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and expressive.

eyes.text: The eyes respond to light and the baby blinks.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby responds to voices and music.

limbs.text: Movements are strong but more limited as space tightens.

muscle.text: Muscles strengthen and tone improves.

bone.text: Bones harden while the skull stays soft for birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys filter and produce urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system continues developing.

lungs.text: The lungs are maturing well and producing more surfactant.

skin.text: The skin is soft, smooth, and filling out with fat; vernix thickens.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The lungs are maturing well, an important step toward breathing after birth.
motherChanges: You may feel increasingly tired with more pressure low in the pelvis. Movements are still important to track.
prenatalQuestions: How will my baby's position be checked?
What signs of labour should I watch for?
Is my blood pressure normal?
tips: Keep tracking your baby's movements daily.
Rest and elevate your feet for swelling.
Begin preparing your hospital bag.
variationNote: Lung maturity progresses on its own timeline, continuing through the final weeks.

sizeSummary: About the size of a honeydew melon, with developing kidneys complete.

brain.text: The brain grows rapidly, putting on weight in these final weeks.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system matures, refining reflexes for life after birth.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports active growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and rounded with fat.

eyes.text: The eyes respond to light and the baby blinks.

ears.text: Hearing is well developed; the baby responds to familiar voices.

limbs.text: Movements are firm but more like squirms as space tightens.

muscle.text: Muscles are well developed and strong.

bone.text: Bones harden while the skull stays soft for birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys are fully developed and producing urine well.

digestive.text: The digestive system is nearly ready to handle milk.

lungs.text: The lungs continue maturing toward independent breathing.

skin.text: The skin is smooth and pink as fat continues to build.

placenta.text: The placenta efficiently supports the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The kidneys are fully developed and the brain is gaining weight quickly.
motherChanges: Your bump is large and space is tight, so movements feel different. You may feel more pelvic pressure.
prenatalQuestions: What is my baby's estimated position and size?
What should I include in my birth plan?
When should I call about contractions?
tips: Finalise your hospital bag and birth preferences.
Rest often and stay hydrated.
Keep monitoring movement patterns.
variationNote: Babies vary in size at this stage; estimates from scans are approximate.

sizeSummary: About the size of a head of romaine lettuce, getting ready for birth.

brain.text: The brain continues maturing rapidly in the final weeks.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines reflexes like grasping and sucking.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports the final stage of growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and rounded with fat.

eyes.text: The eyes respond to light and the baby blinks.

ears.text: Hearing is fully developed; the baby knows your voice.

limbs.text: Movements feel like rolls and stretches as space is limited.

muscle.text: Muscles are strong, including those for sucking.

bone.text: Bones are firm while the skull stays soft to ease birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys are fully working and producing urine.

digestive.text: The digestive system is nearly ready to handle feeding.

lungs.text: The lungs are almost ready to breathe air.

skin.text: The skin is smooth and plump; much of the lanugo has shed.

placenta.text: The placenta continues supporting the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby is getting into position for birth, and the lungs are nearly ready.
motherChanges: If the baby drops lower, you may breathe more easily but feel more pelvic pressure. Weekly checks usually begin.
prenatalQuestions: Is my baby head-down and ready for birth?
What are the early signs of labour?
When should I go to the hospital?
tips: Review the signs of labour with your provider.
Keep your hospital bag and documents ready.
Rest and conserve energy for the days ahead.
variationNote: Whether and when the baby drops varies, and it can happen close to labour.

sizeSummary: About the size of a bunch of Swiss chard — now considered early term.

brain.text: The brain and nervous system continue maturing toward birth.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong, coordinated movement.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system refines reflexes the baby needs for feeding.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports the final stage of growth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and rounded with fat.

eyes.text: The eyes respond to light and the baby blinks.

ears.text: Hearing is fully developed.

limbs.text: Movements are firm rolls and stretches in a snug space.

muscle.text: Muscles are strong, including the grasp and sucking reflexes.

bone.text: Bones are firm while the skull stays soft to ease delivery.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys are fully working.

digestive.text: The digestive system is ready to handle breast milk.

lungs.text: The lungs are continuing to mature, almost ready for air.

skin.text: The skin is smooth and plump with fat.

placenta.text: The placenta continues supporting the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby is now early term, with reflexes like grasping and sucking well developed.
motherChanges: You may notice signs that labour is approaching, like increased pressure or a mucus discharge.
prenatalQuestions: What are the clear signs that labour has started?
When exactly should I head to the hospital?
Is the baby in a good position?
tips: Watch for signs of labour and keep contacts handy.
Rest as much as you can.
Stay hydrated and eat nourishing meals.
variationNote: Full term spans several weeks, so going into labour now or in a few weeks is normal.

sizeSummary: About the length of a leek, with a firm grasp.

brain.text: The brain and nervous system continue maturing.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong movement and reflexes.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system is well developed and refining its reflexes.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation supports the final growth before birth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and rounded.

eyes.text: The eyes are ready to see, with vision that is blurry at birth.

ears.text: Hearing is fully developed.

limbs.text: The baby has a firm grasp and moves in a snug space.

muscle.text: Muscles are strong and ready for feeding and movement.

bone.text: Bones are firm while the skull stays soft for birth.

liver.text: The liver continues maturing.

kidney.text: The kidneys are fully working.

digestive.text: The digestive system is ready for feeding.

lungs.text: The lungs are nearly ready to breathe air independently.

skin.text: The skin is smooth and plump; most lanugo has shed.

placenta.text: The placenta continues supporting the baby.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby has a firm grasp and is nearly ready to meet you.
motherChanges: You may feel very ready and notice more frequent practice contractions and pelvic pressure.
prenatalQuestions: How will I know the difference between false and true labour?
What is my plan if my water breaks?
Is everything ready for delivery?
tips: Keep your phone charged and contacts ready.
Rest and eat well to keep your energy up.
Review your birth preferences once more.
variationNote: The exact day of birth is unpredictable, and most babies arrive within a window around the due date.

sizeSummary: About the size of a small watermelon — now full term.

brain.text: The brain continues developing and will keep growing after birth.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord supports strong movement and reflexes.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system is well developed for life outside the womb.

heart.text: The heart beats strongly and steadily.

circulation.text: Circulation is ready to adapt to breathing air at birth.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and rounded.

eyes.text: The eyes can focus on objects close to the face.

ears.text: Hearing is fully developed.

limbs.text: Movements are firm but limited in the snug space.

muscle.text: Muscles are strong and ready for feeding and movement.

bone.text: Bones are firm while the skull stays soft to ease birth.

liver.text: The liver is ready to take over its full work after birth.

kidney.text: The kidneys are fully working.

digestive.text: The digestive system is ready for milk feeds.

lungs.text: The lungs are mature and ready to breathe air.

skin.text: The skin is smooth and plump, with a protective layer of vernix.

placenta.text: The placenta continues supporting the baby until birth.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord delivers strong blood flow.

milestone: The baby is full term, with mature lungs ready to breathe air.
motherChanges: You may feel impatient and uncomfortable. Watch closely for the signs of labour.
prenatalQuestions: What happens if I go past my due date?
When should I come in once contractions begin?
What are the signs I should call right away?
tips: Rest and conserve energy for labour.
Keep hydrated and eat light, nourishing meals.
Stay calm and trust your body and your care team.
variationNote: A healthy pregnancy can deliver any time around the due date, before or shortly after.

sizeSummary: About the size of a small pumpkin — your due date is here.

brain.text: The brain is fully formed and will continue developing for years to come.

spinalCord.text: The spinal cord is fully developed and supports strong reflexes.

nervousSystem.text: The nervous system is ready to manage life outside the womb.

heart.text: The heart is ready to adapt to breathing air at birth.

circulation.text: Circulation is set to change as the baby takes its first breath.

face.text: Facial features are fully formed and rounded.

eyes.text: The eyes can focus on objects close by and respond to light.

ears.text: Hearing is fully developed and ready for the world.

limbs.text: The baby is fully grown, ready to stretch out after birth.

muscle.text: Muscles are strong and ready for feeding and movement.

bone.text: Bones are firm while the skull stays soft to ease delivery.

liver.text: The liver is ready to take over its full functions.

kidney.text: The kidneys are fully working.

digestive.text: The digestive system is ready for milk feeds.

lungs.text: The lungs are fully mature and ready for the first breath.

skin.text: The skin is smooth and plump, with a protective coating of vernix.

placenta.text: The placenta has supported the baby throughout and will be delivered after birth.

umbilicalCord.text: The umbilical cord has nourished the baby and will be clamped at birth.

milestone: Your baby is fully developed and ready to be born — every organ system is prepared for life outside.
motherChanges: You have reached your due date. You may feel a mix of excitement, tiredness, and anticipation.
prenatalQuestions: What happens if I go a little past my due date?
What are the clear signs to come to the hospital now?
How will labour progress be monitored?
tips: Stay calm and rest between contractions.
Keep your care team's contacts close.
Trust your body and the support around you.
variationNote: Only a small share of babies arrive exactly on the due date — a healthy birth can happen across a window of weeks.
